title: Subway Tile Bookcase
summary: A tall cubby bookcase whose face-framed dividers lay out like a subway-tile grid. Pocket-hole construction; the face frames do double duty as structure and style.
description:
This is a tall 1x12 bookcase divided into a grid of cubbies that reads like a wall of subway tile — a clean, offset pattern of shelves and vertical dividers wrapped in face frames. The cleverness is that the face frames aren't just cosmetic: on a bookcase whose back isn't fully panelled, they're what keep the whole thing from racking. So the 'style' and the 'structure' are the same 1x2 pieces.

Build the basic carcass first — shelves between two sides. Pocket holes are the right call here specifically because the back is open in the cubby areas; screws through the back would have nothing to bite. If you don't have a pocket-hole jig, 2" countersunk screws and glue work, they just show more.

The dividers between shelves are glued and nailed and are not structural, so they can go in quickly. Then the face frames — front and back both — beef the case up and lock the grid. Build the frames first if you have a jig, then attach with glue and 1-1/4" nails; otherwise run the long pieces first, then fit the short ones. Small 1/4" plywood backs go behind the middle cubbies to finish them and add a little more rigidity. Paint suits the tile look.

tools.note: The carcass and face frames both rely on it; the open back leaves nothing else to screw into.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Front and back face frames and trim.

steps.title: Build the basic carcass
steps.body: Build the basic bookshelf by attaching the shelves between the two sides. Use pocket holes — the back isn't fully covered, which helps prevent racking but also means screws through the back would have nothing to grip. If you don't have a jig, 2" countersunk screws and glue will do; they just show. Keep the whole box square.

steps.title: Add the vertical dividers
steps.body: With the basic box built, add the dividers between the shelves to create the cubby grid. The top divider is taller than the rest. These are glued and nailed in place — they're not structural, so they go in quickly and don't need pocket holes.

steps.title: Add the front and back face frames
steps.body: Face frames on both the front and back beef up the bookcase and lock the grid against racking. If you have a jig, build the frames first, then attach with glue and 1-1/4" nails. Otherwise attach the long pieces first, then fit the short ones between them. Nail and glue the frames to every shelf and the top divider too, so the whole thing ties together.

steps.title: Add cubby backs and finish
steps.body: Add the 1/4" plywood backs to the middle cubbies with glue and 3/4" nails — short nails are ideal here so nothing blows through the thin plywood. Fill nail holes, sand, and paint. Paint suits the subway-tile look better than stain, which would fight the clean grid.

images.alt: Tall white bookcase with a subway-tile grid of cubbies
